era_context: The late 12th century saw the Crusader states still dominant in the Levant, though Saladin was consolidating power in Egypt. In Europe, Henry II's Angevin realm was at its height. The Jin Dynasty ruled northern China while the Song Dynasty held the south. Islamic civilization was experiencing intellectual and cultural flourishing despite political fragmentation.

events.title: Murder of Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury
events.description: On December 29, 1170, Thomas Becket, the Archbishop of Canterbury, was murdered in Canterbury Cathedral by four knights: Reginald FitzUrse, Hugh de Morville, William de Tracy, and Richard le Breton. The knights allegedly acted on the wishes of King Henry II, who had reportedly exclaimed in frustration, 'Will no one rid me of this turbulent priest?' (or similar words). Becket had been in conflict with Henry II over the rights and privileges of the Church versus royal authority. The murder shocked Christendom and made Becket a martyr. He was canonized in 1173 and became one of the most important saints in medieval Christianity. The murder also damaged Henry II's reputation and led to his public penance.
key_figures: Henry II (King of England, implicated in the murder)
Reginald FitzUrse, Hugh de Morville, William de Tracy, Richard le Breton (the four knights)

sources.name: William of Canterbury, Life of Thomas Becket

events.certainty_note: The murder of Thomas Becket on December 29, 1170 is one of the most well-documented events of the medieval period. Multiple contemporary sources confirm the date and basic facts.

events.title: Saladin consolidates power in Egypt
events.description: In 1170, Saladin (Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub) continued to consolidate his power in Egypt. He had been appointed vizier of Egypt in 1169 and was systematically eliminating rival factions and strengthening Ayyubid control. By 1170, Saladin's authority in Egypt was becoming unchallenged. His consolidation of power in Egypt was essential to his subsequent military campaigns against the Crusaders. Saladin's rise represented a shift from fragmented regional powers to unified Islamic leadership.
key_figures: Al-Adid (Fatimid Caliph, nominal ruler of Egypt)

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of the Levant
Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin

events.certainty_note: Saladin's consolidation of power in Egypt in 1170 is well-documented in Islamic sources.

events.title: Henry II of England at the height of his power
events.description: In 1170, King Henry II of England was at the height of his power and authority. He controlled vast territories stretching from Scotland to the Pyrenees, making him one of the most powerful rulers in Western Europe. His administrative reforms had strengthened royal authority, and his legal innovations were reshaping English governance. However, the murder of Thomas Becket in December 1170 would damage his reputation and lead to his public penance, marking the beginning of a decline in his authority.
key_figures: Henry II (King of England, Duke of Normandy, Count of Anjou)
events.certainty_note: Henry II's dominance in 1170 is well-documented in contemporary English and Norman chronicles.

events.title: Crusader states maintain dominance in the Levant
events.region: Crusader states (Kingdom of Jerusalem, County of Tripoli, Principality of Antioch)
events.description: In 1170, the Crusader states in the Levant remained the dominant military and political powers in the region. The Kingdom of Jerusalem, the County of Tripoli, and the Principality of Antioch controlled key fortifications and trade routes. However, Saladin's consolidation of power in Egypt was beginning to pose a threat to Crusader dominance. The next few years would see a dramatic reversal of Crusader fortunes.
events.certainty_note: The political dominance of the Crusader states in 1170 is well-documented in contemporary Crusader and Islamic sources.

events.title: Song Dynasty maintains southern Chinese territories
events.description: In 1170, the Song Dynasty under Emperor Xiaozong continued to govern southern China, maintaining a precarious peace with the Jin Dynasty in the north. The Song Dynasty had adapted to its reduced territorial status since the Jin conquest of northern China in 1127. This period saw continued cultural and economic development in the Song south, including advances in printing, ceramics, and maritime trade.

sources.name: Song Dynasty historical records and chronicles

events.certainty_note: General political stability of the Song Dynasty in this period is well-attested, though specific events for 1170 are not prominently documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The murder of Thomas Becket is sometimes portrayed as a spontaneous act of violence, but modern scholarship suggests that Henry II may have deliberately engineered Becket's death, or at least that the knights understood his wishes clearly. The exact words Henry II allegedly spoke are disputed; contemporary sources vary in their accounts. The portrayal of Becket as a simple martyr for Church rights is complicated by the fact that he had previously been Henry II's loyal chancellor and had shifted his allegiance to the Church only after becoming Archbishop. Henry II's public penance for Becket's murder is sometimes exaggerated in popular accounts; while he did perform penance, he was not as thoroughly discredited as some sources suggest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1170 is good, with the murder of Thomas Becket being one of the most well-documented events of the medieval period. However, other events for this year are less detailed. The year is primarily documented through the continuation of ongoing processes (Saladin's consolidation, Henry II's dominance, Crusader control) and the single dramatic event of Becket's murder. Asian sources are primarily from Chinese and Jurchen records. Sub-Saharan Africa and much of Eastern Europe are virtually absent from contemporary written records. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites and religious institutions.

graph_edges.note: Becket's murder led to his canonization and the establishment of his shrine as a major pilgrimage site
Saladin's consolidation in 1170 enabled the elimination of the Fatimid Caliphate in 1171
Saladin's consolidation of power in Egypt enabled his victory at Hattin in 1174
Becket's murder damaged Henry II's reputation despite his political dominance
